questions.question: Which of the following predicate logic formulae/formula is/are CORRECT representation(s) of the statement: "Everyone has exactly one mother"? The meanings of the predicates used are: mother(y,x) : y is the mother of x; noteq(x,y) : x and y are not equal. A. for all x, exists y, exists z (mother(y,x) ^ ~mother(z,x))  B. for all x, exists y [mother(y,x) ^ for all z (noteq(z,y) -> ~mother(z,x))]  C. for all x, exists y [mother(y,x) -> exists z (mother(z,x) ^ ~noteq(z,y))]  D. for all x, exists y [mother(y,x) ^ ~exists z (noteq(z,y) ^ mother(z,x))]
questions.to_find: Which formulae correctly encode 'everyone has exactly one mother'

understand.plain: "Exactly one" is two claims rolled into one: existence (at least one mother) and uniqueness (no second, different mother). For every person x we must say there is a mother y, AND any z that is also a mother of x is forced to be y itself. The correct options are the ones that pin down both halves; an option that only asserts a mother exists, or that gets the logical connective wrong, fails.

keywords.explain: A statement 'there is exactly one t with property R' means there is some t with R(t) and every t' with R(t') equals t. The first conjunct gives existence, the second gives uniqueness.
keywords.example: Exactly one mother y of x: mother(y,x) holds, and any z with mother(z,x) must satisfy z = y.

keywords.explain: Saying 'no z different from y also qualifies' is the same as 'every z different from y is disqualified'; these two phrasings are logically equal by the quantifier-negation rule ~exists z phi = for all z ~phi.
keywords.example: ~exists z (z != y ^ mother(z,x)) equals for all z (z != y -> ~mother(z,x)).

keywords.explain: The leading 'for all x, exists y' fixes one mother y per person x; the inner 'for all z' or '~exists z' must sit inside the y-scope so uniqueness is judged against that very y.
keywords.example: In B, the inner for-all-z is nested under exists y, so z is compared to the chosen mother y.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='518' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='34' class='lbl ink'>Exactly one mother of x</text><text x='20' y='70' class='lbl ink'>= EXISTENCE  +  UNIQUENESS</text><rect x='20' y='90' width='230' height='110' class='hi'/><text x='35' y='118' class='sub ink'>EXISTENCE</text><text x='35' y='146' class='sub ink'>exists y: mother(y,x)</text><text x='35' y='185' class='tag ok'>at least one mother</text><rect x='270' y='90' width='230' height='110' class='hi'/><text x='285' y='118' class='sub ink'>UNIQUENESS</text><text x='285' y='146' class='sub ink'>for all z (z!=y -> ~mother(z,x))</text><text x='285' y='185' class='tag ok'>no second mother</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two panels showing 'exactly one mother' splits into an existence claim (some y is the mother) and a uniqueness claim (every z different from y is not the mother).

given.aim: Identify every option that faithfully captures both existence and uniqueness of a mother for each person.

terms.meaning: Predicate asserting that y is the mother of x
terms.example: mother(Anita, Ravi) means Anita is Ravi's mother
terms.connects: Existence is exists y mother(y,x); uniqueness restricts any other mother to equal y

terms.meaning: Predicate asserting z and y are not equal (z != y)
terms.example: noteq(z,y) is false exactly when z is the same individual as y
terms.connects: Used to single out a 'different' candidate z when testing uniqueness

terms.meaning: The universal that ranges over all people, making the claim hold for everyone
terms.example: for all x ... ensures the property is about every person, not a specific one
terms.connects: Wraps the existence-plus-uniqueness body for each individual x

terms.meaning: exists y gives the one mother; the inner for-all-z or ~exists-z enforces no different second mother
terms.example: B uses for all z(z!=y -> ~mother(z,x)); D uses ~exists z(z!=y ^ mother(z,x))
terms.connects: These two inner forms are logically equivalent by quantifier negation

given.plan: Translate 'exactly one mother' as exists y [mother(y,x) ^ (every other z is not a mother of x)], then test each option for both the existence conjunct and a correctly nested uniqueness clause.

steps.title: Write the target meaning
steps.formula_raw: for all x, exists y [mother(y,x) ^ for all z (noteq(z,y) -> ~mother(z,x))]
steps.apply: Every x has a mother y, and any z different from y fails to be a mother of x. This is exactly Option B, so B is correct.
steps.note: The conjunction '^' between existence and uniqueness is essential; an implication here would not assert that a mother exists.

steps.title: Convert uniqueness to the '~exists' form
steps.apply: By ~exists z phi == for all z ~phi and ~(p -> q) == p ^ ~q, the uniqueness clause of B rewrites to ~exists z (noteq(z,y) ^ mother(z,x)). Substituting gives exactly Option D, so D is correct.
steps.note: B and D are two surface forms of the same statement, hence both are accepted in this multiple-select question.

steps.title: Reject A and C
steps.apply: A says there exist y and z with mother(y,x) ^ ~mother(z,x): it only claims one mother exists and some individual z is not a mother, which any person with a mother satisfies trivially; it does not forbid a second mother. C uses an implication mother(y,x) -> exists z(mother(z,x) ^ ~noteq(z,y)); its antecedent can be made false by choosing a non-mother y, so the whole formula is vacuously true and asserts neither existence nor uniqueness. Both fail.
steps.note: Replacing the existence-uniqueness conjunction with a weak existential (A) or an implication (C) is the classic trap in 'exactly one' translations.

solution.result: Options B and D are correct representations.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the 'exactly one' template exists y [P(y) ^ for all z(z != y -> ~P(z))], whose uniqueness clause is interchangeable with ~exists z(z != y ^ P(z)) by quantifier negation, making B and D equivalent and both correct.

questions.question: Let P(x) be an arbitrary predicate over the domain of natural numbers. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE? A. (P(0) ^ (for all x [P(x) => P(x+1)])) => for all x P(x)  B. (P(0) ^ (for all x [P(x) => P(x-1)])) => for all x P(x)  C. (P(1000) ^ (for all x [P(x) => P(x-1)])) => for all x P(x)  D. (P(1000) ^ (for all x [P(x) => P(x+1)])) => for all x P(x)
questions.answer: A. (P(0) ^ (for all x [P(x) => P(x+1)])) => for all x P(x)
questions.to_find: Which implication is a valid principle over the natural numbers

understand.plain: The domain is the natural numbers 0, 1, 2, 3, ... A true statement here must let you reach EVERY natural number starting from the given base case using the given step. The principle of mathematical induction does exactly this: a base case at 0 plus a step that climbs from x to x+1 covers all of N. The other options either start too high or step in the wrong direction, leaving infinitely many numbers unproven.

keywords.explain: If P(0) holds (base case) and P(x) => P(x+1) holds for every x (inductive step), then P(x) holds for all natural numbers x.
keywords.example: Prove 0+1+...+n = n(n+1)/2: check n=0, then assume for n and derive it for n+1.

keywords.explain: A base case plus a step is only useful if repeatedly applying the step from the base visits every element of the domain; over N the upward step from 0 does, but a downward step or a high start does not.
keywords.example: From P(0) and P(x)=>P(x+1) you get P(0),P(1),P(2),... = all of N.

keywords.explain: An implication is false if you can pick a P making the premises true but the conclusion false; one bad P that the premises allow is enough to defeat it.
keywords.example: For D, let P(x) be 'x >= 1000': premises hold but P(0) is false, so for all x P(x) fails.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='218' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='34' class='lbl ink'>Domain N: 0, 1, 2, 3, ...</text><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='16' class='hi'/><text x='53' y='96' class='sub ink'>0</text><circle cx='150' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='143' y='96' class='sub ink'>1</text><circle cx='240' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='233' y='96' class='sub ink'>2</text><circle cx='330' cy='90' r='16' class='bg'/><text x='323' y='96' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='420' y='96' class='lbl mut'>...</text><line x1='76' y1='90' x2='134' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='166' y1='90' x2='224' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='256' y1='90' x2='314' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='346' y1='90' x2='404' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='30' y='130' class='sub ok'>base P(0)</text><text x='150' y='150' class='tag ink'>step P(x) => P(x+1) climbs over all of N</text><text x='20' y='195' class='big ok'>Option A is valid induction</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A number line of N starting at 0 with arrows P(x) to P(x+1) showing that a base at 0 plus an upward step reaches every natural number, which is option A.

given.aim: Decide which of the four implications is logically valid for every predicate P on the natural numbers.

terms.meaning: The set of natural numbers {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}, the universe over which x ranges
terms.example: x - 1 at x = 0 leaves N, which is why a downward step stalls at the bottom
terms.connects: The base case and step must together cover this entire set

terms.meaning: The single value at which P is asserted outright (here P(0) or P(1000))
terms.example: P(0) anchors induction at the smallest element of N
terms.connects: The step propagates the base case to other elements

terms.meaning: The universally quantified implication that moves truth from one number to a neighbor
terms.example: P(x) => P(x+1) moves up; P(x) => P(x-1) moves down
terms.connects: Combined with the base, it determines which numbers get covered

terms.meaning: The target claim that P holds at every natural number
terms.example: Reached only when base + step visit every element of N
terms.connects: True only for option A; the others leave gaps

given.plan: Recognise the shape of mathematical induction (base at the minimum element plus an upward step) and accept that option; for the rest, exhibit a predicate P that satisfies the premises but fails the conclusion.

steps.title: Match the induction principle
steps.formula_raw: (P(0) ^ for all x (P(x) => P(x+1))) => for all x P(x)
steps.apply: Option A is precisely the principle of mathematical induction over N: the base case P(0) plus the upward step P(x) => P(x+1) forces P(0), P(1), P(2), ... and hence for all x P(x). Therefore A is TRUE.
steps.note: 0 is the least natural number, so starting at 0 and stepping upward leaves no element uncovered.

steps.title: Break B, C, D with counterexamples
steps.apply: B: take P(x) = 'x = 0'. Then P(0) is true and P(x) => P(x-1) holds vacuously for x = 0 (and for x > 0 the antecedent P(x) is false), so the premises hold, yet for all x P(x) is false. C: take P(x) = 'x <= 1000'. P(1000) is true and the downward step P(x) => P(x-1) holds, so the premises hold, but P(1001) is false, so the conclusion fails. D: take P(x) = 'x >= 1000'. P(1000) is true and the upward step P(x) => P(x+1) holds, so the premises hold, but P(0) is false, so the conclusion fails.
steps.note: Each wrong option either starts above 0 or steps the wrong way, leaving infinitely many naturals unproven.

solution.result: Option A is the only TRUE statement; it is the principle of mathematical induction.

questions.formula_note: The valid statement is exactly mathematical induction: base P(0) plus the upward step P(x) => P(x+1) reaches all of N, whereas a higher base or a downward step (B, C, D) leaves infinitely many naturals unproven.

questions.question: Let a(x,y), b(x,y) and c(x,y) be three statements with variables x and y chosen from some universe. Consider the following statement: (exists x)(for all y)[(a(x,y) ^ b(x,y)) ^ ~c(x,y)]. Which one of the following is its equivalent? A. (for all x)(exists y)[(a(x,y) v b(x,y)) -> c(x,y)]  B. (exists x)(for all y)[(a(x,y) v b(x,y)) ^ ~c(x,y)]  C. ~(for all x)(exists y)[(a(x,y) ^ b(x,y)) -> c(x,y)]  D. ~(for all x)(exists y)[(a(x,y) v b(x,y)) -> c(x,y)]
questions.answer: C. ~(for all x)(exists y)[(a(x,y) ^ b(x,y)) -> c(x,y)]
questions.to_find: An equivalent rewriting of the given quantified formula

understand.plain: We are given exists x, for all y [(a ^ b) ^ ~c] and asked which option means the same thing. The trick is to push a negation through the quantifiers and connectives: flipping every quantifier and negating the body once, then negating again outside, lands you back on the original. The implication form (a ^ b) -> c is the negation of (a ^ b) ^ ~c, so an outer '~' on the all/exists version recovers exactly our statement.

keywords.explain: ~for all y phi = exists y ~phi and ~exists x phi = for all x ~phi; pushing a negation inward swaps each quantifier to its dual and negates the inside.
keywords.example: ~for all x exists y R(x,y) = exists x for all y ~R(x,y).

keywords.explain: ~(p -> q) = p ^ ~q, and equivalently p -> q = ~p v q; this links the 'implication' options to the 'conjunction' original.
keywords.example: ~((a^b) -> c) = (a^b) ^ ~c, which is the body of our statement.

keywords.explain: The options carry a leading '~'; an option equals the original exactly when stripping its outer '~' produces the negation of the original, because ~(negation) = original.
keywords.example: If S is the original, then ~(~S) = S, so the right option is '~(negation of S)'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='558' height='218' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='38' class='sub ink'>Original S:  exists x, for all y [(a^b) ^ ~c]</text><rect x='20' y='58' width='520' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='35' y='84' class='sub ink'>negate inward: for all x, exists y [(a^b) -> c]</text><rect x='20' y='112' width='520' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='35' y='138' class='sub ink'>this block = ~S  (the negation of S)</text><text x='20' y='185' class='lbl ok'>So S = ~(for all x, exists y [(a^b) -> c])  =  Option C</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A flow showing the original existential statement negated inward to a universal-implication block, which equals the negation, so prefixing it with '~' recovers the original (option C).

given.aim: Find the option logically equivalent to S = (exists x)(for all y)[(a ^ b) ^ ~c].

terms.meaning: Three binary predicates over a common universe; only their logical combination matters here
terms.example: a ^ b is true when both hold; ~c is true when c fails
terms.connects: The body (a^b) ^ ~c is the negation of (a^b) -> c

terms.term: Quantifier prefix exists x, for all y
terms.meaning: There is some x such that for every y the body holds
terms.example: Negating it yields for all x, exists y with the body negated
terms.connects: Determines how an outer negation redistributes over the quantifiers

terms.meaning: The matrix of the formula; equals ~((a ^ b) -> c)
terms.example: When (a^b) is true and c is false, the body is true
terms.connects: Its negation (a^b) -> c is what appears inside the correct option C

terms.meaning: The leading negation in options C and D; applying it to ~S returns S
terms.connects: Makes ~(negation of S) the equivalent form we seek

given.plan: Compute the negation ~S by flipping quantifiers and turning the body (a^b)^~c into (a^b)->c, then note that prefixing this with one more '~' gives back S, matching option C.

steps.title: Negate S by pushing '~' inward
steps.formula_raw: ~(exists x)(for all y) phi = (for all x)(exists y) ~phi
steps.apply: Take the negation of S: ~S = ~(exists x)(for all y)[(a^b) ^ ~c] = (for all x)(exists y) ~[(a^b) ^ ~c]. Flip exists x to for all x and for all y to exists y, carrying the negation inside.
steps.note: Each quantifier turns into its dual exactly once as the negation moves past it.

steps.title: Simplify the negated body to an implication
steps.apply: By De Morgan, ~[(a^b) ^ ~c] = ~(a^b) v ~~c = ~(a^b) v c, which is exactly (a^b) -> c. Hence ~S = (for all x)(exists y)[(a^b) -> c].
steps.note: Recognising ~(p ^ ~q) = p -> q is the step that produces the implication form.

steps.title: Recover S with an outer negation
steps.apply: Since ~S = (for all x)(exists y)[(a^b) -> c], applying double negation gives S = ~(~S) = ~(for all x)(exists y)[(a^b) -> c]. This is exactly Option C. (Options A, B use a^b replaced by a v b or drop the negation, and D uses a v b, so none of them equals S.)
steps.note: The discriminator between C and D is the inner connective: the original has a ^ b, so the equivalent must keep a ^ b, ruling out D's a v b.

solution.result: Option C, ~(for all x)(exists y)[(a^b) -> c], is equivalent to the given statement.

questions.formula_note: Pushing a negation through the prefix (exists x, for all y -> for all x, exists y) and rewriting the body via ~((a^b) ^ ~c) = (a^b) -> c shows S equals ~(for all x, exists y[(a^b) -> c]); the surviving a ^ b (not a v b) singles out option C over D.

formulas.name: Unique Existence (Exactly One) in First Order Logic
formulas.plain: exists y [P(y) ^ for all z (z != y -> ~P(z))]
formulas.whenToUse: Use to translate 'there is exactly one t with property P' into predicate logic: assert existence of a witness y and that every other z fails P.

terms.means: the property required to hold of the unique object y
z is a candidate different from the witness y
any different z does not satisfy P, enforcing uniqueness

formulas.trap: The connective between existence and uniqueness must be conjunction (^); using an implication makes the formula vacuously true and loses the existence claim. The uniqueness clause for all z(z!=y -> ~P(z)) is equivalent to ~exists z(z!=y ^ P(z)).
formulas.reference: Standard first order logic encoding of the uniqueness quantifier exists!

formulas.name: Quantifier Negation (De Morgan for Quantifiers)
formulas.plain: ~for all x phi = exists x ~phi ; ~exists x phi = for all x ~phi
formulas.whenToUse: Use to push a negation across a quantifier: each quantifier flips to its dual and the negation moves onto the inner formula.

terms.means: universal quantifier; its negation is exists x
existential quantifier; its negation is for all x
the formula in the scope of the quantifier that gets negated

formulas.trap: Only the outermost quantifier flips per step; apply the rule repeatedly for nested quantifiers, and remember to negate the entire body, not just one quantifier.
formulas.reference: De Morgan laws extended to quantifiers in predicate logic

formulas.name: Negation of an Implication
formulas.latex:
eg(p ightarrow q) \equiv p \wedge 
eg q, \qquad (p ightarrow q) \equiv 
eg p \vee q
formulas.whenToUse: Use to convert between an implication and a conjunction/disjunction, e.g. when negating a conditional or rewriting a conjunction with a negated part as an implication.

terms.means: material implication, true unless p is true and q is false

formulas.trap: ~(p -> q) is p ^ ~q, NOT ~p -> ~q or p -> ~q; the negation keeps p true and forces q false.
formulas.reference: Material implication and its negation in propositional logic

formulas.name: Principle of Mathematical Induction
formulas.plain: (P(0) ^ for all x (P(x) => P(x+1))) => for all x P(x)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to prove a property holds for every natural number: establish the base case P(0) and the inductive step P(x) => P(x+1).

terms.means: base case, the property at the least natural number 0
inductive step carrying the property from x to its successor
conclusion that P holds for every natural number

formulas.trap: The base must sit at the bottom of the domain and the step must climb upward; a higher base (P(1000)) or a downward step (P(x)=>P(x-1)) over N leaves infinitely many values uncovered and is invalid.
formulas.reference: Peano induction over the natural numbers
